questions.question: What causes a merge conflict in Git?
options: Two branches modify the same lines of the same file
A branch is deleted before merging
questions.answer: Two branches modify the same lines of the same file

questions.question: Which conflict marker indicates the start of YOUR current branch's version?
What separates your current branch's changes from the incoming branch's changes in a conflict marker?
In the conflict markers, what appears after the >>>>>>> line?
options: The name of the incoming branch being merged
The commit hash of the merge base
questions.answer: The name of the incoming branch being merged

questions.question: When Git encounters a merge conflict, what happens to the merge process?
options: Git automatically picks the newer version
The merge is paused and needs your help to resolve
Git reverts both branches to their common ancestor
questions.answer: The merge is paused and needs your help to resolve

questions.question: Can Git automatically merge changes when two branches modify different files?
options: No, all merges require manual resolution
Yes, Git can automatically merge changes to different files
Only if the files are in the same directory
Only if both branches were created at the same time
questions.answer: Yes, Git can automatically merge changes to different files

questions.question: Can Git automatically merge changes to different parts of the same file?
options: No, any change to the same file causes a conflict
Yes, Git can merge changes to different parts of the same file
Only if the file is a text file
Only if both changes are additions, not deletions
questions.answer: Yes, Git can merge changes to different parts of the same file

questions.question: How does 'git status' describe a file with a merge conflict?
questions.answer: both modified

questions.question: After resolving a conflict in a file, what is the FIRST step to complete the merge?
options: Stage the resolved file with git add
questions.answer: Stage the resolved file with git add

questions.question: After staging a resolved conflict file, what do you do next to finish the merge?
options: Run git commit with a merge message
questions.answer: Run git commit with a merge message

questions.question: What type of commit is created when you resolve a merge conflict and complete the merge?
questions.answer: A merge commit

questions.question: When resolving a conflict, what must you remove from the file?
options: The entire file contents and start over
questions.answer: All conflict markers: <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>>

questions.question: Which command creates and switches to a new branch in one step?
questions.answer: git switch -c new-branch

questions.question: When resolving a conflict, are you required to pick only one side's changes?
options: Yes, you must pick either yours or theirs
No, you can combine both changes or write something entirely new
Yes, Git enforces choosing one side
No, but only if you use git merge --both
questions.answer: No, you can combine both changes or write something entirely new

questions.question: What command do you use to abort a merge that has conflicts?
questions.answer: git merge --abort

questions.question: What happens to your working directory after running 'git merge --abort'?
options: Everything returns to the state before the merge attempt
The conflicting files are moved to a backup directory
questions.answer: Everything returns to the state before the merge attempt

questions.question: Which command shows a visual branch graph in the terminal?
questions.answer: git log --oneline --graph --all

questions.question: In the Day 11 exercise, what file was used for the initial single-file conflict practice?
questions.answer: shared.txt

questions.question: If two branches modify line 5 of a file but do NOT modify line 10, will line 10 cause a conflict?
options: Yes, any file with a conflict has all lines conflicted
No, only the lines where changes overlap will cause a conflict
Yes, because the file is marked as both modified
It depends on the branch that was created first
questions.answer: No, only the lines where changes overlap will cause a conflict

questions.question: When merging branch-b into main and both branches created header.txt and content.txt with different contents, how many files have conflicts?
options: It depends on the file size
questions.answer: Two files

questions.question: When resolving multiple file conflicts in a single merge, how do you stage them?
options: You can only stage one file at a time with separate commits
You stage all resolved files with git add and then make one merge commit
You must run git merge --continue after each file
Git stages them automatically once you remove the markers
questions.answer: You stage all resolved files with git add and then make one merge commit

questions.question: In the conflict markers block, the content between <<<<<<< HEAD and ======= represents what?
options: The original version before either branch changed it
The version on your current branch
The version from the branch being merged in
questions.answer: The version on your current branch

questions.question: In the conflict markers block, the content between ======= and >>>>>>> feature-branch represents what?
options: The original version before either branch changed it
The version on your current branch
The version from the branch being merged in
questions.answer: The version from the branch being merged in

questions.question: What does a merge commit record?
options: That both branches were combined and any conflicts were resolved
That the repository was cloned from a remote
questions.answer: That both branches were combined and any conflicts were resolved

questions.question: After aborting a merge with 'git merge --abort', is any data lost?
options: Yes, all commits on the incoming branch are deleted
Yes, the conflicting file is permanently corrupted
No, everything is back to the way it was before the merge attempt
No, but the incoming branch is locked from future merges
questions.answer: No, everything is back to the way it was before the merge attempt
